content: Standard python approaches to measure fiber/thread diameter perpendicular to local direction from a binary mask, in increasing robustness: (1) naive distance-transform doubling (cv2.distanceTransform, width = 2x distance-to-background at centerline) — simple but only exactly correct for locally-straight, symmetric cross-sections; breaks down near curvature/endpoints. (2) skeletonize + multiply by distance map (pixel intensity at skeleton = local radius) — same idea, standard combo (quanfima package uses this for fiber analysis in biomaterials). (3) True perpendicular ray-casting: at each medial-axis point, cast a ray perpendicular to the *local tangent direction* of the skeleton, find the two mask-boundary intersection points, measure the distance between them — this is the only method that is actually robust to curvature, and is the closest computational analog to what a human does when hand-tracing perpendicular edges in ImageJ. Distance-transform-only methods systematically over- or under-estimate width on curved segments because they assume local symmetry.
